next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

Gavin woke to the sound of pipes gurgling in the walls. The kitchens beneath the Hufflepuff dormitories ran early on Christmas morning, and the warm-water pipes that cut through the stone behind his headboard ticked and knocked as they heated. It was the only sound. The other four beds in the room were empty except for Malcolm Preece's, and Malcolm was still asleep, his blankets pulled up to his eyebrows with one foot sticking out at the bottom, bare and pale.

There was snow against the window. Not the charmed interior snow that sometimes drifted through the common room, but real snow piled against the ground-level glass, dimming the light to a blue-grey wash. The fire had gone out overnight. Gavin's breath showed when he sat up.

At the foot of his bed, three things. His mother's parcel, wrapped in brown paper and tied with the same string she used to bundle newspapers for recycling, her handwriting neat and small on the tag. A package from Miren, soft, wrapped in pages torn from the Daily Prophet — she'd used the employment section, which was the only part that didn't carry war news these days. And a card from his aunt in the cream-coloured envelope she always used.

He opened his mother's parcel in bed, sitting cross-legged with the blankets over his shoulders. A jumper — hand-knitted, dark blue, too large. She always knitted them too large because she was convinced he was growing faster than he was. A tin of fudge with a note that said *Merry Christmas love Mum and Dad, the cat knocked over the tree again, Dad fixed it with a Sticking Charm so now we can'...
open_hooks_to_carry: The routine of solitary work: translation at night, failed First Art practice, the undramatic frustration of not being talented enough for what he is attempting
The Transfiguration mock failure: his Switching Spell turns a teacup into a lump of clay. His teacher attributes the failure to trauma. Gavin does not correct this impression
prev_tail_excerpt:
...given the same information, would conclude exactly what they had concluded — that a stressed boy under an authoritarian regime had found a fixation that was metabolising his anxiety into grand cosmic narratives.

They were both wrong, too. The wards were real. The degradation was real. The outside was real and patient and vast and it did not care about the wards or about him or about anything, because caring was not a thing it did, the way seeing was not a thing a stone did. It simply occupied its space the way it always had and always would, without effort, without awareness, without anything at all.

He went to bed. He lay in the dark and listened to his dormitory mates breathing in their sleep — the small, ordinary sounds of fifteen-year-olds who had enough to be frightened of already without knowing the rest. Somewhere above and beyond and outside, the wards held. Thinner than yesterday. Thinner than the day before that. And the thing beyond them — the condition, the environment, the default state of a reality in which magic had never been more than a local exception — was there the way it had always been there. Not waiting, because waiting was a thing that minds did. Just there. The way the ocean is there whether the seawall knows it or not.

Gavin lay on his back and stared at the underside of his bed canopy and knew that nobody was going to help him, and that he was not good enough to do it alone, and that it was going to have to be him anyway because there was nobody else, and after a while he closed his eyes and did not sleep and did not sleep and eventually slept.
state_deltas_required: The grinding daily texture of working alone under occupation — classes that feel irrelevant, practice that yields no results, guilt about Duncan that produces no action, the slow accumulation of isolation

Gavin commits to the work and accepts isolation. He fails his Transfiguration mock OWL. His cowardice regarding Duncan is visible and unresolved. The chapter establishes the novel's emotional baseline: ordinary life under occupation, colored by obsession and moral failure
